MINUTES FOR DEC-17-2020 9:30 AM

LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE MAY-25-2021 HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS DEXTER CHAN AND APRIL CHAN'S PETITION FOR APPOINTMENT OF NEUTRAL ARBITRATOR, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. BY JUNE 1, 2021, DEFENDANTS MAY FILE AND SERVE A LIST OF THREE PROPOSED ARBITRATORS. THE COURT THEREAFTER WILL FOLLOW THE PROCEDURE SPECIFIED IN CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 1281.6: IT WILL ISSUE AN ORDER NOMINATING FIVE PERSONS FROM THE PARTIES' LISTS OR, IF DEFENDANTS FAIL TO TIMELY SUBMIT A LIST OF PROPOSED ARBITRATORS, FROM OTHER SOURCES. WITHIN FIVE DAYS OF RECEIPT OF NOTICE OF THE NOMINEES FROM THE COURT, THE PARTIES MUST JOINTLY SELECT THE ARBITRATOR, WHETHER OR NOT THE ARBITRATOR IS AMONG THE NOMINEES. IF THE PARTIES FAIL TO DO SO, THE COURT WILL APPOINT THE ARBITRATOR FROM THE NOMINEES. ORDER SIGNED. (SEE SIGNED ORDER FOR COMPLETE RULING.) JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: SEAN KANE; NOT REPORTED (302/EPS)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-09-2021 Continued To Jun-29-22 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Motion To Compel Consumer Arbitration And To Strike Unconscionable Clauses
Off Calendar Nov-08-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Robert Hernandez, Esq. (626.383.6014) of Fiduciary Law Attorneys for Plaintiff.
No other appearances.

Not argued. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTs DEXTER CHAN and APRIL CHAN's Motion To Compel Consumer Arbitration And To Strike Unconscionable Clauses is denied.

On December 17, 2020, this Court granted Defendants' motion to compel arbitration. After Defendants filed a petition to appoint a neutral arbitrator, the Court granted that petition, and the parties subsequently notified the Court that they had jointly selected an arbitrator to hear the matter. Defendants now complain that Plaintiff has engaged in "bad faith bargaining" by refusing to agree to have the same neutral serve as both mediator and arbitrator. They also complain that they were "deceived" into signing the arbitration agreement and that the agreement is "unconscionable." Finally, they seek to challenge determinations made by JAMS with respect to the rules that will govern the arbitration.

The short answer to all of these arguments is that the Court has previously enforced the arbitration agreement at Defendants' own request. It is far too late for Defendants to have second thoughts. Moreover, this Court lacks jurisdiction to either remake the parties' bargain or to tell JAMS or the arbitrator how to proceed. "Once a dispute is submitted to arbitration, the [California Arbitration Act] contemplates limited, if any, judicial involvement. 'Typically, those who enter into arbitration agreements expect that their dispute will be resolved without necessity for any contact with the courts. It is the job of the arbitrator, not the court, to resolve all questions needed to determine the controversy." (Briggs v. Resolution Remedies (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1395, 1400 (citations and quotations omitted) [holding that trial court lacked the authority to review a discretionary prehearing order of an arbitrator].) "An arbitration has a life of its own outside the judicial system. The trial court may not step into a case submitted to arbitration and tell the arbitrator what to do and when to do it: it may not resolve procedural questions, order discovery, determine the status of claims before the arbitrator or set the case for trial because of a party's alleged dilatory conduct." (Titan/Value Equities Group, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 29 Cal.App.4th 482, 487-489 [rejecting argument that the court has power to intervene on behalf of parties "stalled in arbitration," holding that it is "up to the arbitrator, and not the court, to grant relief for delay in bringing an arbitration to a resolution"]; see also Brock v. Kaiser Foundation Hospitals (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 1790, 1796 ["Once a court grants the petition to compel arbitration and stays the action at law, the action at law sits in the twilight zone of abatement with the trial court retaining merely a vestigial jurisdiction over matters submitted to arbitration"].) For similar reasons, Plaintiff's requests for sanctions for Defendants' refusal to pay their share of the arbitration fee to JAMS and for terminating sanctions are denied. The action is stayed pending the completion of the arbitration. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1281.4.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-05-2021 Continued To Dec-29-21 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Petition For Appointment Of Neutral Arbitrator
Off Calendar May-25-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS DEXTER CHAN AND APRIL CHAN's Petition For Appointment Of Neutral Arbitrator.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

By order filed December 17, 2020, the Court granted defendants' motion to compel arbitration under their fee agreement with plaintiff attorney. The agreement provides, "The parties shall appoint one person or agree upon a 3-person panel to hear and determine the dispute, except that if the parties cannot agree, then the Superior Court of San Francisco shall choose an impartial arbitrator whose decision shall be final and conclusive on all parties." Plaintiff shows that she has proposed that Defendants select one of two alternative dispute resolution organizations, JAMS or ADR Services, or propose their own forum, and that Defendants have not responded. Plaintiff also shows that on January 15, 2021, she proposed three arbitrators at JAMS, and that on February 26, 2021, Defendants responded, "We found your list unacceptable and we are not limiting our search to any specific organization." Defendants have not proposed any arbitrators, and their attempts to schedule the arbitration before an arbitrator or a forum have been selected are premature.

In the interest of moving the arbitration forward, the Court rules as follows: By June 1, 2021, Defendants may file and serve a list of three proposed arbitrators. The Court thereafter will follow the procedure specified in Code of Civil Procedure section 1281.6: it will issue an order nominating five persons from the parties' lists or, if defendants fail to timely submit a list of proposed arbitrators, from other sources. Within five days of receipt of notice of the nominees from the Court, the parties must jointly select the arbitrator, whether or not the arbitrator is among the nominees. If the parties fail to do so, the Court will appoint the arbitrator from the nominees.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-01-2020 Continued To Jan-27-21 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
Off Calendar Oct-05-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Emilie M. Calhoun (415-759-8755; ecalhoun@fiduciary-law.com) of FIDUCIARY LAW ATTORNEYS, Plaintiff.
Dexter Chan (dexter@me.com) and April Chan (abchan411@gmail.com), Defendants in propria persona.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTs DEXTER CHAN and APRIL CHAN's MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND:

Defendants' motion to set aside the default entered against them on June 22, 2020 and default judgment entered on July 20, 2020 is granted.

"Aside from statute, courts have an inherent power to correct judgments where there has been a clerical error by the clerk . . . There is no time limit within which this correction need be made . . . [T]he power to amend . . . extends . . . to cases where some provision of . . . an order or judgment as made or rendered was due to the inadvertence or mistake of the court and may therefore properly be treated as a clerical misprision." (Morgan v. State Bd. of Equalization (1949) 89 Cal.App.2d 674, 677.) Pursuant to the COVID-19 emergency rules, the Presiding Judge issued general orders deeming March 18, 2020 to June 1, 2020 a court holiday for purposes of computing time for filing papers, and extended the time period to answer. As a result, Defendants' responsive pleading was not due until 30 days after June 1, and it therefore was an error for default to be entered on June 22.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
